statement: The EU Treaty framework imposes specific post-employment obligations on senior EU officials. Article 245 TFEU (Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union) states that members of the European Commission shall, both during and after their term of office, 'respect the obligations arising therefrom and in particular their duty to behave with integrity and discretion as regards the acceptance, after they have ceased to hold office, of certain appointments or benefits.' The European Commission's Code of Conduct for Commissioners (revised 2018) operationalises this: departing Commissioners face a 2-year cooling-off period (3 years for the Commission President) during which they must notify the Commission of any intended new professional activity and obtain approval. If the activity relates to their former portfolio, it is presumptively prohibited during the cooling-off period. The European Ombudsman has investigated several cases of alleged revolving-door violations, most notably the move of former Commission President José Manuel Barroso to Goldman Sachs in 2016, which led to the strengthening of the code in 2018. Despite these rules, enforcement remains contested — the Commission's own ad hoc ethics committee assesses notifications, but critics argue it lacks independence. The European Parliament has repeatedly called for the establishment of an independent EU ethics body.
source_name: Article 245 TFEU; European Commission Code of Conduct for Commissioners (C(2018) 700); European Ombudsman Decision OI/1/2021/KR
caveats: EU revolving-door rules apply to Commissioners and senior EU staff, not to member state officials in Council formations or national representatives. If Iceland joined the EU, its Commissioner would be subject to these rules, but Icelandic ministers attending Council meetings would not. The rules have been criticised both as too weak (by transparency NGOs like Transparency International EU) and as too intrusive (by those who argue they deter qualified candidates from serving). The 2018 strengthening was partly a response to the Barroso controversy but has not been tested in a comparable high-profile case since.

related_entries.statement: Iceland has an established legal framework for managing conflicts of interest in government. Key instruments include: (1) Lög um varnir gegn hagsmunaárekstrum í Stjórnarráðinu (Act on Prevention of Conflicts of Interest in Government Offices), which requires disclosure of financial interests and imposes restrictions on officials' participation in decisions where they have personal interests; (2) Siðareglur ráðherra (Code of Ethics for Ministers), which establishes standards of conduct for cabinet members; (3) General administrative law principles on disqualification (vanhæfi) under the Administrative Procedures Act (stjórnsýslulög nr. 37/1993). However, Iceland does not have a post-employment cooling-off period requirement for ministers or senior officials — there is no mandatory waiting period before former officials can take positions in the private sector or with international organisations. This gap has been highlighted in international assessments, including GRECO (Group of States against Corruption) evaluation reports.

Many Western democracies have stricter post-employment (revolving door) restrictions than Iceland for senior government officials. Examples include: France requires a 3-year cooling-off period for senior officials before joining companies they supervised, enforced by the Haute Autorité pour la Transparence de la Vie Publique (HATVP); Canada imposes a 2-year post-employment restriction for ministers and 1 year for senior officials, enforced by the Office of the Conflict of Interest and Ethics Commissioner; the European Commission imposes a 2-year cooling-off period for departing Commissioners (extended to 3 years for the Commission President), during which they must notify and receive approval for any new professional activities; the United Kingdom has an Advisory Committee on Business Appointments (ACOBA) that reviews post-employment moves by ministers and senior officials, typically recommending 6–24 month waiting periods (though compliance is voluntary). By contrast, Iceland has no mandatory cooling-off period for ministers or senior officials, a gap highlighted in GRECO evaluations.

Sjálfstæðisflokkurinn (Independence Party) introduced a bill in Althingi proposing an 18-month cooling-off period that would prevent senior officials and ministerial advisors involved in negotiations with international organisations from accepting positions with those organisations after leaving office. The bill was introduced in the context of potential EU accession negotiations, framed as a safeguard against conflicts of interest. Similar 'revolving door' regulations exist at the EU institutional level: EU Commissioners face an 18-month cooling-off period (extended to 24 months for the Commission President), and the European Ombudsman has called for stricter enforcement. Several EU member states, including France and Germany, have analogous rules for senior civil servants.
